content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


June 11, 2001


Marlene L. Garcia
California Legislature
Senate Office of Research
1020 N Street, Suite 200
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-083

Dear Ms. Garcia:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the gift and
honoraria provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. May you accept a $1,500 stipend for the work you perform for the National
Center for Public Policy and Higher Education Associate's Program for 2001-
2002?

2. May you accept reimbursement for travel and related expenses for your
participation in the program?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. To the extent your participation in the program over the course of the year
provides the National Center with greater or equal consideration for the stipend,
the payment is considered earned income, which must be reported on your statement of
economic interests.

_________________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. 01-083
Page No. 2

2.      Yes. Reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide
charitable organization is not "income" within the meaning of the Act, nor is it a
"gift" if the recipient provides consideration of equal or greater value to the
organization.

FACTS

You are writing to request advice regarding your participation in the National
Center for Public Policy and Higher Education's Associates Program.

The National Center for Public Policy and Higher Education ("the National
Center" or "Center") is an independent, nonprofit organization exempt from taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The National Center conducts
independent research and analysis of pressing policy issues facing the states and the
nation regarding opportunity and achievement in higher education, and works to improve
higher education through effective public policy.

You have been selected, along with 12 other candidates from around the country,
to participate in the National Center's Associate Program. During the next year,
associates in this national policy program will be asked to review specific materials
provided by the Center in order to fully participate in three, two to three-day private
policy symposia (one in Virginia and two in California) discussing issues related to
higher education. In addition, associates will be asked to periodically review and
comment on draft National Center papers, project descriptions, research findings, etc.
Communication between the policy symposia is to take place mainly via scheduled
conference calls, e-mails and circulation of documents. Candidates may also have the
opportunity to become more involved in the work of the National Center by serving on
advisory groups to National Center projects or conducting research and analysis. These
opportunities will be negotiated individually between the National Center and the
associate involved. The National Center will provide an annual stipend of $1,500 to each
candidate, payable in three equal disbursements, and will reimburse all travel and related
expenses for participation in this program.

You are currently a designated employee with the Senate Office of Research.
You believe the National Center selected you based on your higher education policy
expertise, which you have developed over the course of your 20-year professional career,
and not based on your status as a public official. You are the only legislative staff
member selected to participate in the program, and with the exception of one individual,
all other associates work within higher education settings.

You would like to know whether you may accept the $1,500 stipend and the
reimbursements for travel and related expenses for your participation in this program.

File No. 01-083
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The characterization of your anticipated payments from the National Center as
honoraria, income or gifts must be determined in order to decide whether you may accept
them, and whether they must be reported. As a designated employee of the Senate Office
of Research with full disclosure obligations, you are required by the Act to report income
and gifts over a certain amount on your statement of economic interests. (See Sections
82019 and 87206-87210.) In addition, as applied to officials in your capacity, the Act
prohibits the receipt of honoraria (Section 89502) and limits gifts to $320 per calendar
year from a single source. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.)

Honoraria.

Section 89502(c) provides that no designated employee of a state agency shall
accept an honorarium from any source if the employee would be required to report the
receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her Statement of Economic Interests.
Section 89501(a) generally defines honorarium as ". . .any payment made in
consideration for any speech given, article published, or attendance at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal or like gathering."

Although you will occasionally be called upon to attend and participate in private
symposia, you will also be reviewing and commenting on draft National Center papers,
project descriptions and research findings, and serving on advisory groups or conducting
research and analysis on National Center projects. It is evident from the facts presented
that the payment for your work as a National Center Associate does not meet the
definition of "honorarium," because the services you will provide the Center include
more than making a speech, writing an article, or attending a meeting or a public or
private conference. (Rainey Advice Letter, No. A-98-101, Fessler Advice Letter, No. I-
93-408; Regulation 18931.3.) The $1,500 stipend is to compensate you for the totality of
the services you will provide the National Center during the 2001-2002 Associates
Program, not merely for your attendance at the symposia. On these grounds, we
conclude the stipend you will receive in connection with this work is not an honorarium.
When activities for which a person is compensated do not meet the definition of
honorarium, the payment may be considered either a gift or income, depending on
whether equal or greater consideration is provided for the payment. (Sections 82028 and
82030.)

Gifts

Section 82028 defines a gift as ". . . any payment that confers a personal benefit
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received . . . ." (Emphasis added.) As stated above, you will receive a $1,500 stipend
disbursed in three $500 payments. These payments are to compensate you for the entire
range of services you provide the Center during the 2001-2002 Associates Program.
Additional payments may also be made to reimburse you for travel and related expenses
for your participation in the program. It is clear from the facts presented that the service
you will provide the Center as an associate is consideration given in exchange for the

File No. 01-083
Page No. 4

$1,500 stipend. In addition, payments to reimburse your travel and related expenses are
specifically tied to your participation as an associate in the program. Accordingly, the
stipend and reimbursements for travel and expenses will not meet the definition of "gift"
under the Act if you determine the services you provide are of equal or greater value than
the amount of payment(s) you receive from the Center.

If an official claims that a payment for travel is not a gift, the official has the
burden of proving that he or she provided consideration of equal or greater value in
return. (Section 82028; Allen Advice Letter, No. A-95-188; Fessler Advice Letter,
supra; Chang Advice Letter, No. I-92-410.) Equal consideration means that each party
receives benefits of equal value. (In re Burciaga (1975) 2 FPPC Ops. 17.) While we
have no exact formula to determine whether the official has provided consideration of
equal or greater value, the following general guidelines may be of assistance. The value
of services rendered may be proven by evidence as to the customary rate of compensation
for such services, irrespective of official status. (Matulich Advice Letter, No. I-97-304
and Allen Advice Letter, No. A-96-181.) Also relevant in the determination might be the
length of time spent rendering the services, or whether the services are of the type not
readily available from others. (Allen Advice Letter, No. A-96-121.) Ultimately,
however, the determination of whether equal consideration has been provided is
necessarily a factual one.

Income.

Section 82030 generally defines income as a payment received, including but not
limited to any salary, wage, advance, reimbursement for expenses or per diem, where
consideration of equal or greater value is provided to the source of the payment. Income,
other than a gift does not include income from any source outside the jurisdiction and not
doing or planning to do business in the jurisdiction. Because the National Center is
located and does business in California, the $1,500 stipend you will receive for your
services will be considered reportable income, and not a gift, to the extent you are able to
demonstrate you have provided consideration of equal or greater value in return for the
payment(s).

There is an exception to income for payments made by a bona fide nonprofit
entity exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code to
reimburse travel expenses and per diem. (Section 82030(b)(2).) We have advised that
this exception does not apply to gifts of travel (i.e., travel payments for which equal or
greater value was not provided by the recipient). (Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-89-200;
Quam Advice Letter, No. A-89-182.) As stated above, travel reimbursements you receive
from the Center will not be considered gifts within the meaning of the Act if you provide
consideration of equal or greater value. In that case, this exception will apply and
reimbursement from the National Center for your travel and related expenses for
participation in the 2001-2002 Associate Program will not be considered income because
the Center is a nonprofit organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of
the Internal Revenue Code. (Section 89506)(a)(2).)

File No. 01-083
Page No. 5

As a designated employee with the state Senate Office of Research, you have full
disclosure obligations. Therefore, you are required to report all gifts valued at $50 or
more and all sources of income of $500 or more to you that do business in California on
your next statement of economic interests. You are not required to report travel
reimbursements that are neither income nor gifts under the Act. Also, the Act's conflict-
of-interest provisions (see Sections 87100 and 87103), will preclude you for 12 months
after receiving income of $500 or more from the National Center, from making,
participating in or using your legislative staff position to influence government decisions
which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial impact upon the Center.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Margaret E. Figeroid
Legal Division
